section.title: Your Steadfast Love, O Lord
section.sectionRationale: A psalm of David contrasting the self-deceived heart of the wicked, who have no fear of God before their eyes, with the LORD's loving devotion reaching to the heavens, His righteousness like the mountains, and the fountain of life and light found in Him alone.
section.thesis: The self-deceived wickedness that has no fear of God before its eyes stands utterly overmatched by the LORD's loving devotion, which reaches to the heavens and holds the very fountain of life and light for all who take refuge in Him.
section.frameNote: Three movements: an oracle describing the wicked man's self-deceived heart, with no fear of God before his eyes (1-4); the LORD's expansive loving devotion, faithfulness, and righteousness, culminating in the fountain of life and light (5-9); and a closing prayer for continued loving devotion and protection from the proud (10-12).

units.label: No fear of God before his eyes

verses.text: An oracle is in my heart regarding the transgression of the wicked man: There is no fear of God before his eyes.
For his eyes are too full of conceit to detect or hate his own sin.
The words of his mouth are wicked and deceitful; he has ceased to be wise and well-doing.

Even on his bed he plots wickedness; he sets himself on a path that is not good; 
he fails to reject evil.

units.label: With You is the fountain of life

verses.text: Your loving devotion, O LORD, reaches to the heavens, Your faithfulness to the clouds.

Your righteousness is like the highest mountains; Your judgments are like the deepest sea. 
O LORD, You preserve man and beast.

How precious is Your loving devotion, O God, that the children of men take refuge 
in the shadow of Your {{kanaph|wings}}!
They feast on the abundance of Your house, and You give them drink from Your river of delights.
For with You is the {{mekor|fountain}} of life; in Your {{or|light}} we see light.
Extend Your loving devotion to those who know You, and Your righteousness to the upright in heart.
Let not the foot of the proud come against me, nor the hand of the wicked drive me away.
There the evildoers lie fallen, thrown down and unable to rise.

kanaph.gloss: Wing, edge, extremity. The image of taking refuge in the shadow of God's wings pictures the tender, protective care of a parent bird, echoed in Ruth 2:12 and taken up by Jesus longing to gather Jerusalem under His wings, Matthew 23:37.

mekor.gloss: Fountain, spring, source. David locates the very source of life itself in God, an image Jesus fulfills by offering living water that becomes in believers a spring welling up to eternal life, John 4:14.

or.gloss: Light. David declares that in God's light, humanity sees light itself, an image fulfilled in Jesus, the light of the world, in whom was life, and that life was the light of men, John 1:4, 8:12.

basic.context: David describes an inward oracle about the wicked man's heart, so full of conceit that it cannot even detect its own sin, plotting wickedness even while at rest. Against this bleak portrait, David sets the LORD's loving devotion, reaching to the heavens, His righteousness like the highest mountains and His judgments like the deepest sea, under whose wings the children of men find refuge, feasting on abundance and drinking from a river of delights, for with God is the very fountain of life and light. The psalm closes with a prayer that this loving devotion would continue toward those who know Him, protecting them from the proud and the wicked, who will themselves finally fall.
basic.hermeneutics: This psalm should be read as a study in contrast, moving deliberately from the claustrophobic, self-deceived interior world of the wicked to the vast, cosmic expanse of God's loving devotion, teaching that no human wickedness, however self-assured, can compare to the scale of God's character.
basic.originalLanguages: David pictures the children of men taking refuge in the shadow of God's kanaph, wings, feasting at His house and drinking from His river, for with Him is the mekor, fountain, of life, and in His or, light, all humanity sees light itself.
basic.typology: The fountain of life and the light in which all light is seen, verse 9, anticipates Jesus, in whom was life, and that life was the light of men, John 1:4, who offers living water that becomes a spring welling up within believers to eternal life, John 4:14, and who declares Himself the light of the world, John 8:12.
basic.christ: Jesus is the fountain of life and the light of the world this psalm celebrates, fulfilling verse 9 completely: 'In him was life, and the life was the light of men,' John 1:4. He offers the living water David only pictures as a river of delights, promising that whoever drinks it will never thirst again, a spring welling up to eternal life, John 4:14. And the refuge David finds in the shadow of God's wings, verse 7, is echoed in Jesus's own lament over Jerusalem, longing to gather her children as a hen gathers her brood under her wings, Matthew 23:37.
basic.god: God is shown in cosmic, overwhelming terms, His loving devotion reaching to the heavens and His righteousness like the highest mountains, yet intimately near, sheltering the children of men under His own wings and satisfying them from the fountain of life itself.
basic.crossReferences: John 1:4 identifies Jesus as the life that is the light of men, fulfilling this psalm's picture of the fountain of life and light. John 4:14 shows Jesus offering living water that becomes an internal, eternal spring, fulfilling the river of delights this psalm describes. Romans 3:10-18 quotes language echoing verse 1 as part of Paul's case for universal human sinfulness, showing the desperate need every person has for the loving devotion this psalm celebrates.
basic.oneStory: David's contrast between the self-deceived heart of the wicked and the LORD's loving devotion, reaching to the heavens and holding the fountain of life and light, finds its complete fulfillment in Jesus Christ, in whom is life and light for the world, and who offers living water that satisfies forever all who take refuge in Him.

context.literary: A psalm of David built on a deliberate contrast, moving from a compact, almost claustrophobic portrait of the wicked heart, verses 1-4, to the cosmic scale of God's loving devotion, verses 5-9, before closing in personal prayer, verses 10-12.
context.historical: Likely composed by David reflecting on the character of persistent oppressors he had encountered, using their self-deception as a foil to magnify, by contrast, the vastness of God's own faithful character.
context.canonical: Its opening description of human wickedness is echoed in Paul's catena of Old Testament quotations proving universal sinfulness, Romans 3:10-18, while its climactic verse 9 anticipates the New Testament's fullest revelation of life and light in Jesus Christ, John 1:4.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose the self-deceiving nature of persistent, unrepentant wickedness and to magnify, by deliberate contrast, the vast and intimate loving devotion of God, who is the true fountain of life and light for all who take refuge in Him.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes both the character of the wicked and the character of God; it functions prescriptively as an invitation to examine one's own heart for self-deception and to seek refuge and satisfaction in God rather than in wickedness that masquerades as wisdom.

debates.question: Does the description of the wicked man in verses 1-4 depict a particular class of especially hardened oppressors David observed, or a universal portrait of human sinfulness that applies, apart from grace, to every person?

views.label: The passage describes a specific type of persistent, unrepentant oppressor David had directly observed, a particular class of especially hardened wrongdoers, rather than a universal claim about every human heart without exception
views.case: The psalm's language, 'the wicked man,' reads naturally as a specific character study, similar to other wisdom psalms contrasting the righteous and the wicked as distinguishable types within the community, rather than a claim about universal human nature.

views.label: Language echoing this passage, 'no fear of God before their eyes,' is quoted by Paul in Romans 3:18 as part of a catena proving that every person, Jew and Gentile alike, is under sin, suggesting the psalm's description, while occasioned by specific observation, ultimately captures a universal truth about the human heart apart from grace
views.case: Paul's use of this language in Romans 3 to argue that 'none is righteous, no, not one,' shows that the New Testament reads such descriptions of the wicked as disclosing something true of fallen human nature generally, not merely a description of a distant, separate class of especially bad people.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that self-deceived wickedness is a real and serious danger; whether the psalm's original scope was a specific class of oppressors or a universal portrait, its New Testament use in Romans 3 confirms that every person needs the loving devotion and light this psalm ultimately celebrates in God alone.

study.originalLanguages: The children of men take refuge in the shadow of God's kanaph, wings, feasting at His house and drinking from His river, for with Him is the mekor, fountain, of life, and in His or, light, all humanity sees light itself, fulfilled completely in Jesus Christ.

typology.type: The fountain of life and light, verse 9
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, in whom was life, and the life was the light of men, John 1:4, 8:12.

typology.type: Refuge in the shadow of God's wings, verse 7
typology.fulfillment: Jesus longing to gather Jerusalem's children under His wings as a hen gathers her brood, Matthew 23:37.

study.christ: Jesus is the fountain of life and the light of the world this psalm celebrates, fulfilling verse 9 completely: 'In him was life, and the life was the light of men,' John 1:4. He offers the living water David only pictures as a river of delights, a spring welling up within believers to eternal life, John 4:14, and declares Himself the light of the world, John 8:12. The refuge David finds in the shadow of God's wings, verse 7, is echoed in Jesus's own lament over Jerusalem, longing to gather her children under His wings, Matthew 23:37.
study.god: God is shown in cosmic, overwhelming terms, His loving devotion reaching to the heavens and His righteousness like the highest mountains, yet intimately near, sheltering the children of men under His own wings and satisfying them from the very fountain of life.

crossReferences.note: Jesus identified as the life that is the light of men, fulfilling this psalm's picture of the fountain of life and light in God.
Jesus offers living water becoming an internal, eternal spring, fulfilling the river of delights this psalm describes.
Paul echoes language from this psalm's opening description of the wicked as part of his case for universal human sinfulness apart from grace.

study.oneStory: David's contrast between the self-deceived heart of the wicked and the LORD's loving devotion, reaching to the heavens and holding the fountain of life and light, finds its complete fulfillment in Jesus Christ, in whom is life and light for the world, offering living water that satisfies forever all who take refuge in Him.

quiz.q: What does verse 1 say is missing from the wicked man's eyes?

options.text: The fear of God.
options.note: The psalm's opening oracle diagnoses the root of wickedness as the absence of the fear of God.

options.text: General moral awareness of right and wrong.
options.note: Close, since moral perception is involved, but the verse specifically names the fear of God, reverence toward Him, not moral awareness considered on its own.

options.text: Knowledge of the law's details.
options.note: The issue described is a lack of reverent fear of God, not merely ignorance of legal details.

options.text: Wealth and possessions.
options.note: The verse concerns the absence of the fear of God, not material lack.

quiz.q: According to verse 2, why can the wicked man not detect or hate his own sin?

options.text: His eyes are too full of conceit, or self-flattery.
options.note: The psalm diagnoses self-deception rooted in pride as the reason wickedness goes unrecognized by the wicked themselves.

options.text: He has never been taught right from wrong.
options.note: The text specifically attributes this blindness to conceit and self-flattery, not simply a lack of education.

options.text: He is simply careless and inattentive to his own actions.
options.note: Close, since inattention is part of the surface picture, but the psalm specifically roots this in conceit, active self-flattery, not mere carelessness.

options.text: God has hidden the truth from him.
options.note: The blindness described is self-inflicted through conceit, not something imposed externally by God.

quiz.q: What three qualities of God does David describe in verses 5-6 using expansive, cosmic imagery?

options.text: His loving devotion, faithfulness, and righteousness, reaching to the heavens, clouds, and mountains.
options.note: David deliberately uses the vastness of creation to picture the scale of God's character.

options.text: His power, wisdom, and knowledge, described in general terms.
options.note: Close, since these are true divine attributes elsewhere, but verses 5-6 specifically name loving devotion, faithfulness, and righteousness, not power, wisdom, and knowledge.

options.text: His wealth, power, and popularity.
options.note: The psalm's imagery concerns God's moral character, not material wealth or popularity.

options.text: His silence, distance, and hiddenness.
options.note: This contradicts the psalm's emphasis on God's expansive, actively displayed character.

quiz.q: What does verse 9 say is found with God?

options.text: The fountain of life, and light by which we see light.
options.note: This verse locates the very source of life and light in God Himself, fulfilled in Jesus, John 1:4.

options.text: The source of all wealth and comfort alone.
options.note: The verse specifically names life and light, a deeper and more foundational reality than material comfort.

options.text: General blessing, without specific mention of life or light.
options.note: Close, since blessing is implied, but verse 9 specifically and vividly names the fountain of life and light, not blessing in vague, general terms.

options.text: A hidden treasure available only to kings.
options.note: The verse describes life and light available to all who take refuge in God, not a treasure limited to royalty.

quiz.q: What does David pray for in verse 10, and what warning closes the psalm in verse 12?

options.text: That God's loving devotion would extend to those who know Him, closing with the image of evildoers fallen and unable to rise.
options.note: The psalm closes contrasting ongoing blessing for the faithful with the final downfall of the wicked.

options.text: That God would simply ignore the wicked rather than actively judging them.
options.note: Close, since the wicked's fate is in view, but verse 12 pictures active downfall, thrown down and unable to rise, not mere divine neglect or indifference.

options.text: That David would receive greater wealth than his enemies.
options.note: The prayer concerns loving devotion and righteousness, not material wealth comparison.

options.text: That the wicked would ultimately triumph.
options.note: This is the opposite of the psalm's closing image, evildoers thrown down and unable to rise.

questions: David diagnoses the wicked as too full of conceit to even detect their own sin. Where might self-flattery be blinding you to a sin you need to honestly confront?

David describes God's loving devotion, faithfulness, and righteousness using the vastness of the heavens, clouds, and mountains. What area of your life feels too small or too broken for God's character to actually reach?
David pictures people feasting at God's house and drinking from His river of delights. Where are you currently seeking satisfaction in lesser sources instead of the fountain of life God offers?
Verse 9 says in God's light we see light itself. How has following Jesus, the light of the world, changed the way you see everything else in your life?

The psalm closes praying that God's loving devotion would extend specifically to those who know Him. How would you describe your own relationship with God right now, someone who merely knows facts about Him, or someone who truly knows Him?

translationNotes.note: An oracle renders a Hebrew term elsewhere used for prophetic utterances, suggesting David's insight into the wicked man's heart carries the weight of divinely given discernment, not mere personal opinion.
The shadow of Your wings is a common Old Testament image of divine protection, echoed in Ruth 2:12 and taken up by Jesus in Matthew 23:37.
Fountain of life renders mekor, a term for a natural spring, source, or origin, here identifying God Himself as the very source from which all life flows.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 36:1-12. No em or en dashes present in the source text or in this file. No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) was found specifically expositing Psalm 36 in searches conducted this session, so sermon status is none.
